sources.dataset: CDC PLACES: Census Tract Data (GIS Friendly Format), 2025 release
sources.note: Model-based small-area CRUDE prevalence (BRFSS), with 95% CIs.

sources.dataset: National age-specific prevalence + adult age distribution (cited constants)
sources.note: NHANES/CDC age-specific rates; ACS/Census age mix.
Per-person incremental annual cost RANGES with citations; never summed.

method: Joined PLACES crude tract prevalence to modeled members; emitted BOTH a naive residential estimate and a workforce-adjusted estimate (A1 age-standardization to the workforce age mix; A2 compositional shrinkage toward the national member mean by λ). Sized each condition as affected × incremental-cost RANGE.
integrity: Cost driver = size of burden, NOT a saving.
Workforce adjustment exposed (toggle + λ documented), not hidden.
Limits stated: area-based LODES, national age rates, partial uniformity capture.

evidence.claim: For a male-skewed workforce, obesity barely tracks income — among men obesity is roughly flat across income (higher for Black men); the income→lower-obesity pattern is mainly a White-women phenomenon. So poor-area members are NOT meaningfully healthier on this measure; obesity is largely environmental.
Income–obesity association differs sharply by sex and race — weak/absent for men.
evidence.source: Income inequality & obesity: Does Sex Matter? (2021)

evidence.claim: Burden is sized on COVERED ADULTS (employees + adult dependents), not just employees — ≈1.9 covered lives per subscriber, ~¼ children → adults ≈ 1.4× participants. Retail/part-time workforces skew lower, so this is an upper estimate; the census (Mode 1B) refines it.

limitations: The workforce adjustment shrinks residential prevalence toward the national member mean, but does NOT apply a LOCAL relative-standing correction. For income-sensitive conditions (diabetes control, hypertension) poor-area burden may be modestly overstated; obesity's weak income gradient limits this for the headline measure.
Member SEX is not modeled (LODES carries no sex). Pulling occupational sex ratios (BLS) would let the obesity gradient be weighted properly — door-open.
The rigorous fix is a joint model where member income/standing drives BOTH burden and engageability consistently (the agent-based roadmap).
